findings.summary: 410 single-line docstrings that restate the function name verb-for-verb, spread across 188 source files. Patterns like 'Get commit history.', 'Clone a repository.', 'List running containers.' convey zero information beyond what the function signature already states.
evidence: git_operations/mcp_tools.py: 11 occurrences — 'Get commit history.', 'Clone a repository.', 'Revert a commit.', 'Stash uncommitted changes.', 'Apply a stash.', 'List all stashes.', 'List all tags.', 'Fetch from remote.', 'Add a remote.', 'Remove a remote.', 'List all remotes.'
agents/history/__init__.py: 10 occurrences of 2-3 word restating verb docstrings
quantum/circuit.py: 8 occurrences, all of the form 'Add X gate.' or 'Get X.'
cache/invalidation/__init__.py: 3 occurrences
cloud/common/__init__.py: 11 occurrences
Grep for pattern '"""(Get|List|Create|Update|Delete|Clone|...) \w+( \w+)?\.'" returns 410 matches across 188 files
findings.suggestion: Replace restating verb docstrings with a sentence that explains *why* or *when* to call this function, describes non-obvious constraints, or documents the return value contract. If no such information exists, delete the docstring entirely — an empty docstring is preferable to noise. Prioritise mcp_tools.py files and agents/history/ as they have the highest density.

findings.summary: 284 test method docstrings of the form 'Verify X behavior.' across 89 test files. These are the replacement form after the earlier 'Test functionality:' mass-replacement and carry equal zero information content. 'Verify submodules behavior.' and 'Verify untrusted value behavior.' describe neither the precondition, the action, nor the expected outcome.
evidence: test_trust_gateway.py: 'Verify untrusted value behavior.', 'Verify verified value behavior.', 'Verify trusted value behavior.', 'Verify three levels behavior.', 'Verify destructive tools count behavior.', 'Verify safe tools count behavior.', 'Verify no overlap behavior.', 'Verify union is complete behavior.'
test_orchestrator_coverage.py: 'Verify every behavior.', 'Verify at behavior.', 'Verify cron behavior.'
test_self_healing.py: 5 occurrences including 'Verify timeout behavior.', 'Verify permission behavior.'
test_module_performance.py: 'Verify function behavior.' appearing on dynamically-generated test methods
Grep for '"""Verify \w+ behavior\.'" returns 284 matches across 89 test files
findings.suggestion: Test docstrings should follow the 'Given/When/Then' or 'does X when Y' pattern. Example: 'Verify untrusted value behavior.' should become 'TrustLevel.UNTRUSTED must serialize to the string "untrusted".' The function name already carries the When; the docstring should state the observable invariant being checked.

findings.summary: Widespread single-line comments that restate the immediately following code without adding intent or constraint information. Patterns like '# Create parent directories' above Path.mkdir(), '# Get current branch' above a git branch call, and '# Set defaults' above simple assignments are noise, not documentation.
evidence: git_operations/core/repository.py: '# Create parent directories', '# Update metadata before cloning', '# Update metadata after successful clone', '# Update metadata to reflect failed clone', '# Get current branch', '# Update local repository information'

git_operations/api/visualization.py: '# Set default report name', '# Create output directory', '# Create comprehensive report', '# Filter results based on user preferences', '# Set defaults' (twice), '# Create visualization', '# Add structure analysis', '# Add commit statistics'

fpf/io/section_manager.py: '# Filter patterns by part', '# Get pattern IDs', '# Filter concepts by pattern IDs', '# Filter relationships where both source and target are in this part', '# Add dependencies if requested', '# Filter patterns'
coding/refactoring/__init__.py: '# Find all identifiers (used)', '# Find the definition (simple variable assignment)', '# Find all usages (excluding the definition)'
findings.suggestion: Delete restating comments. Retain only comments that explain a non-obvious constraint, a business rule, a workaround for an external bug, or the reason a particular approach was chosen over alternatives. 'Why' and 'what would surprise a reviewer' are the tests for a comment's value.

findings.summary: Tests for the MCP bridge and server assert on private instance attributes (_tool_registry, _resources, _prompts), creating hard coupling to implementation layout. Any refactoring of MCPServer's internal field names will silently break these tests without changing observable behavior.
evidence: test_mcp_bridge.py:50: assert len(server._tool_registry.list_tools()) == get_total_tool_count()
test_mcp_bridge.py:56: assert len(server._resources) >= RESOURCE_COUNT
test_mcp_bridge.py:61: assert len(server._prompts) == PROMPT_COUNT
All three assertions access underscore-prefixed attributes that are implementation details of MCPServer, not its public interface
findings.suggestion: Expose a public query API on MCPServer (e.g., server.tool_count(), server.resource_count(), server.prompt_count()) and assert against those. Alternatively, test through the MCP protocol call surface (call_tool, list_resources, list_prompts) rather than asserting on internal storage fields.

findings.summary: Tests pin PROMPT_COUNT == 10 as a literal equality, and similarly pin SAFE_TOOL_COUNT relationships. Every time a new prompt or tool is added, these tests fail without communicating why the count changed. Count tests written this way become maintenance friction rather than safety.
evidence: test_mcp_bridge.py:350: assert PROMPT_COUNT == 10  # 3 original + 7 expansion prompts — literal equality on a count that will change whenever prompts are added
test_mcp_bridge.py:342: assert TOOL_COUNT >= 18 — floor is appropriate; the equality in line 350 is not

test_trust_gateway.py:145: assert len(promoted) == SAFE_TOOL_COUNT — ties verification to an auto-computed constant derived from discovery, but the constant itself has no independent correctness anchor
findings.suggestion: For counts expected to grow, use >= with a documented minimum. For counts that must be exact (e.g., the number of prompts is contractual), add a comment explaining why the number is fixed and what process must be followed to change it. Consider a test that instead verifies each expected prompt exists by name, making additions visible and removals caught by name.

findings.summary: The performance test suite generates test helpers with docstring 'Verify function behavior.' on dynamically created functions and on inner callables. These tests verify timing metrics are positive (> 0) and memory is non-negative (>= 0) — assertions that can never fail under any code change, making the tests formally vacuous.
evidence: test_module_performance.py:319: assert result.status == 'success' and assert result.execution_time > 0 and assert result.memory_usage >= 0 — memory >= 0 can never be False
test_module_performance.py:356-357: assert result.execution_time > 0 and assert result.memory_usage >= 0 — same vacuous memory check
test_module_performance.py:386: assert result.execution_time > 0 — no upper bound, no regression threshold
Performance regression is demoted to a warning rather than a test failure: 'if result.regression_detected: warnings.warn(...)' — this defeats the purpose of a regression test
findings.suggestion: Performance tests should assert against a threshold (e.g., execution_time < N seconds) derived from a recorded baseline, or compare against a known-good reference run. The regression detection logic already exists (result.regression_detected); make it fail the test, not emit a warning. Remove memory_usage >= 0 assertions — they are always true.

findings.summary: desloppify camelCase heuristic triggers on PascalCase class-name references and JavaScript-origin literals within Python strings, producing false positives. No genuine camelCase variable or function naming was observed.
evidence: Pattern MCPToolRegistry, LoadTestResult, AgentMemoryStore — all PascalCase class names, not camelCase variables
isError, isActive, toolName appearing in JSON/MCP protocol payloads within string literals
grep -rn "[a-z][A-Z]" src/ returns only class references, test stub names, and JSON protocol fields
findings.suggestion: No action needed for the false positives. The 12-point deduction (100→88) accounts for the heuristic ambiguity — the codebase is consistently snake_case for variables and functions. If a future refactor touches the MCP protocol layer, ensure that Python identifiers wrapping those protocol fields remain snake_case.
